title: Zero Migration: Preserve Order
description: Move all zeros in the given integer array to the end while preserving the relative order of non-zero elements. Return the resulting array. Constraints: O(1) extra space (in-place) is preferred; input length can be zero; negative numbers allowed; zeros only moved to the end.
hints: Use a two-pointer approach to compact non-zero elements to the front while counting zeros.
Finally, fill the remaining positions with zeros to maintain array length.

complexity.explanation: Single pass to compact non-zeros and a final pass to fill zeros.

title: Peak Parade: Record-Breakers Count
description: Given an array of integers, count how many elements are strictly greater than all previous elements in the array (prefix maximums). Return the count. The function should run in O(n) time and O(1) extra space. If the array is empty, return 0.
hints: Maintain a running maximum as you iterate from left to right.
Increment the count whenever the current element is strictly greater than the running maximum.

solution.javascript: function solve(param) { return result; }
solution.python: def solve(param): return result

complexity.explanation: Single pass with a running maximum; constant extra space.

title: PrefixSum Stopper
description: Compute the sum of the leading elements of the array until a negative value is encountered. If there is no negative value, sum all elements. Edge cases: empty array, negative at first position.
hints: Scan from left to right and accumulate sum until you see a negative.
If you reach the end without encountering a negative, the sum is the total of all elements.

solution.javascript: function solve(param) { var sum = 0; for (let i = 0; i < param.length; i++) { if (param[i] < 0) break; sum += param[i]; } return sum; }

complexity.explanation: Single pass to accumulate until the first negative; constant extra space.

title: Even Orchard: Sum of Even Numbers
description: Given an array of integers, return the sum of all even numbers. If there are no even numbers, return 0. Works with negative numbers and zeros. Time complexity: O(n). Space complexity: O(1).

testCases.description: Basic: mix of odd and even positives

hints: Hint 1: Iterate through all elements and accumulate those divisible by 2.
Hint 2: Negative even numbers should be included in the sum.

complexity.explanation: One pass over the array, accumulate even numbers.

title: Hash Echoes: Distinct Duplicates Counter
description: Given an array of integers, return the number of distinct values that appear more than once in the array. Use a hash-map to count frequencies and then count how many values have frequency > 1. Constraints are typical interview data; aim for O(n) time and O(n) space.
hints: Use a hash-map to count each value's frequency, then count how many frequencies > 1.
Edge cases: empty array, no duplicates, all elements same.

solution.javascript: function solve(param) { const arr = Array.isArray(param) ? param : JSON.parse(param); const freq = new Map(); for (const x of arr) { freq.set(x, (freq.get(x) || 0) + 1); } let count = 0; for (const v of freq.values()) { if (v > 1) count++; } return count; }

complexity.explanation: We traverse the array once to count frequencies using a hash map, then iterate over frequencies to count how many exceed 1.

title: Singletons Sum Challenge
description: Given an array of integers, return the sum of elements that appear exactly once in the array. Implement both JavaScript and Python solutions. Constraints: aim for a solution that runs in O(n) time and uses O(n) extra space for frequency counting. Include 3-4 test cases that cover typical scenarios and edge cases (empty array, all duplicates, negatives/zero, mix of positives and duplicates).
hints: Build a frequency map for all elements, then sum only the elements whose frequency is 1.

complexity.explanation: Two-pass approach: first count frequencies, then sum singleton elements.

title: Sorted Sum Synthesis
description: Given an array of integers, sort it in non-decreasing order and return the sum of all elements. Implement in JavaScript and Python. Constraints: 1 <= N <= 1e5, values fit 32-bit signed integer.
hints: Sort first, then sum.
Be careful with negative numbers when summing.

complexity.explanation: Sorting dominates time; summation is linear; extra space for the sorted copy.

hints: For each row, perform a binary search to find the first and last index of the target.
Sum (lastIndex - firstIndex + 1) across all rows to get the total count.

title: SumBridge Pairs
description: Given a sorted array of integers and a target sum, return the count of unique pairs (i, j) with i < j such that arr[i] + arr[j] == target. A pair is considered unique by value (e.g., (1,3) counted once even if multiple indices contain these values). Constraints: 0 <= arr.length <= 1e5, |arr[i]| <= 1e9, target is an int. Time complexity should be O(n) with O(1) extra space.
hints: Use a left and right pointer starting at the ends of the array.
When a pair sums to target, increment count and move both pointers past all duplicates of those values.

complexity.explanation: Two-pointer sweep from both ends counts unique pairs whose sum equals target, skipping duplicates to ensure uniqueness.

title: Stacked Parser: Infix Expression Evaluator (Two-Stack)
description: Medium difficulty: implement a two-stack infix expression evaluator that handles non-negative integers, + - * /, and parentheses. Whitespace allowed. Division must be integer division (truncate toward zero). Expressions are valid, no unary operators. The challenge is designed to be solvable in 10-20 minutes.
hints: Handle multi-digit numbers and spaces. Build tokens or parse on the fly.
Ensure operator precedence: */ before +-; handle parentheses correctly; division is integer.

complexity.explanation: We parse the expression once, using two stacks (values and operators) to apply operators by precedence, yielding O(n) time and O(n) space.

title: SilentLinkedTotal
description: Calculate the sum of node values in a singly linked list. The input is provided as a JSON array representing node values in order. Implement in JavaScript and Python. Starter function signature is given; return the sum of all values. Do not modify input. Should run in a single pass.
hints: Iterate once through the list and accumulate a running total.
Treat the input as the values of a singly linked list in order.

solution.javascript: function solve(param) { let sum = 0; for (const v of param) { sum += v; } return sum; }

complexity.explanation: One linear pass over the input array; constant extra space.

title: LinkedList Purge (Easy)
description: Given a singly linked list represented as an array of integers, remove all nodes that have the value equal to target. Return the resulting list as a JSON array string. The function solve(param) should accept param as either a two-element array [listArray, target] or a JSON string '[[list], target]'. Implement in JavaScript and Python. Starter code provided. Aim for an O(n) time solution with O(n) space for the output.
hints: Think in terms of filtering the list.
Return the resulting list as a JSON array string.

complexity.explanation: We scan the input list once and build a new list excluding elements equal to target.

title: BlockWiseFlipLinkedList

Time complexity: O(n); Space: O(1).

title: Merge Trio of Sorted Lists
description: Merge K sorted lists (represented as arrays) into a single sorted list. Input is an object with a 'lists' field containing 1..k sorted arrays. Return a single sorted array. Time budget: ~15 minutes. Do not modify the input lists in place.
hints: Consider merging by collecting all values and sorting.
Edge cases: empty lists and negative numbers.

complexity.explanation: Flatten all values from the lists and sort; returns a new sorted array.

title: OddDivisorConsecutiveCount
description: Given a positive integer n, return the number of representations of n as a sum of one or more consecutive positive integers. The count equals the number of odd divisors of n. Implement an efficient O(sqrt(n)) solution. Treat n <= 0 as 0 representations.

testCases.description: Larger number with few odd divisors

hints: The number of representations equals the number of odd divisors of n.
First strip all factors of 2 from n, then count divisors of the remaining odd part.

complexity.explanation: We factor only the odd part of n after removing powers of two; the number of representations equals the product of (exponent+1) for odd prime factors.

title: ConsecutiveSumRepresentations

The solution should run in O(sqrt(n)) time and O(1) space by counting the odd divisors of n (after removing powers of two).

hints: Use prefix sums modulo k to group equal remainders.
For each remainder, number of subarrays is C(freq, 2).

title: OddDivisorEcho
description: Given a positive integer n, count how many representations exist of n as a sum of one or more consecutive positive integers. The number of representations equals the number of odd divisors of n. Implement solve to return the count as a string. The input to solve may be provided as a JSON array containing n or as a plain number; test cases use a JSON-encoded array string to simulate typical competition input.
hints: Hint 1: The number of representations of n as a sum of consecutive positives equals the number of odd divisors of n.
Hint 2: To count odd divisors, remove all factors of 2 from n to obtain the odd part m, then count divisors of m.

complexity.explanation: Remove all factors of 2 to obtain the odd part m; count divisors of m by iterating up to sqrt(m).

title: FirstIndex Seeker
description: Return the 0-based index of the first occurrence of a target in the given array. If not present, return -1. Constraints: linear scan is fine; handle duplicates and edge cases (empty array).
hints: Think about a single pass, compare each element with target and return its index upon first match.
Edge cases: empty array and target not present.

solution.javascript: function solve(param) { const { arr, target } = param; for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) { if (arr[i] === target) return i; } return -1; }

complexity.explanation: Single pass scans from left to right; returns the first matching index; uses constant extra space.

title: Matrix Threshold Counter
description: Given a 2D matrix where each row is sorted in ascending order and each column is also sorted in ascending order, count how many elements are less than or equal to a given target. Implement solve(param) where param is either a JSON string or an object with keys 'matrix' and 'target'. Return the count as an integer.
hints: Count per row: for each row, find how many elements are <= target.
Use binary search on each row to count elements <= target efficiently.

complexity.explanation: For each of the m rows, perform a binary search on the row (log n). Sum results across rows.

title: Lexicon Miner
description: Given a target string s and a dictionary, determine the minimum number of dictionary words needed to form s by concatenation. Return -1 if impossible. Constraints: lowercase letters only; implement dynamic programming over string prefixes; provide implementations in JavaScript and Python.
hints: Use dynamic programming on the prefix length of the string.
For each position i, try every dictionary word that matches starting at i and update the minimal piece count for the endpoint.

complexity.explanation: DP on string prefixes; at each index, try every dictionary word to see if it matches starting at that index and update the best (minimum pieces) for the ending index.

hints: Consider using a binary search to locate the first index i where arr[i] >= target.
Handle edge cases: target smaller than all elements, target larger than all elements, duplicates, and empty arrays.

Compare target with mid and endpoints to decide which half to search next.

title: Stack Rewind: Reverse an Array
description: Given an input array, return a new array with elements in reverse order using a stack. Build the reversal without using built-in reverse methods. Do not mutate the input. Time limit 15 units; memory usage should be O(n).
hints: Push all input elements onto a stack, then pop them to build the reversed array.
Ensure you return a new array (do not mutate the input).

complexity.explanation: All elements are pushed and popped exactly once; extra space is proportional to input size.

title: Postfix Stack Evaluator
description: Evaluate a postfix expression using a stack. The input is a space-separated string of integers and operators (+, -, *, /). Return the integer result. Division truncates toward zero. Implement solve(param).
hints: Hint 1: Use a stack to store operands as you scan tokens from left to right.
Hint 2: On encountering an operator, pop two operands (b and a), compute a <op> b, and push the result.

complexity.explanation: Each token is processed once; the stack stores at most n/2 operands for the worst-case.

title: Stack Navigator: Next Greater Indices
description: Compute, for each element in an array, the index of the next greater element to its right using a stack. Return an array of indices (0-based). If no greater element exists to the right, use -1. Must run in O(n) time and O(n) extra space.
hints: Use a monotonic decreasing stack of indices to track elements whose next greater hasn't been found yet.
When you see a value larger than the value at the index on the stack's top, pop indices and record the current index as their next greater.

solution.javascript: function solve(param) { const a = param; const n = a.length; const res = new Array(n).fill(-1); const stack = []; for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) { while (stack.length && a[i] > a[stack[stack.length - 1]]) { res[stack.pop()] = i; } stack.push(i); } return res; }

complexity.explanation: Traverse the array once while maintaining a stack of indices with unresolved next greater elements. When a larger value appears, pop and assign its next greater index; remaining indices have -1 as their next greater.

title: Stack Longevity: Longest Valid Parentheses
description: Given a string s of '(' and ')', compute the length of the longest well-formed (valid) parentheses substring. Use a stack-based approach with O(n) time and O(n) space. Constraints: 0 <= |s| <= 1e5, only '(' and ')' characters.
hints: Use a stack to store indices of unmatched '(' characters.
Track the length as you traverse; when you encounter ')', pop and compute distance from last unmatched index.
Initialize stack with -1 to handle edge case of valid substring starting at index 0.

complexity.explanation: We traverse the string once, using a stack to track indices of '('; when encountering ')', we pop and compute the length of a valid substring based on the last unmatched index.

title: Two-Character Substring Stretch
description: Given a string s, return the length of the longest substring that contains at most two distinct characters. The string consists of lowercase English letters. Implement a linear-time solution with a sliding window. Provide both JavaScript and Python implementations.

hints: Hint 1: Use a sliding window that expands to the right and contracts from the left when more than two distinct characters are in the window.
Hint 2: Maintain a frequency map of characters currently in the window; when its size exceeds 2, shrink from the left until only two distinct characters remain.

complexity.explanation: Single pass with a small constant-size frequency map (at most 3 keys during resizing) plus a left boundary moving forward.

title: Cosmic Character Census
description: Given a string, return the count of distinct characters in the string. Treat characters case-sensitively and count spaces and punctuation as characters. Constraints: implement efficiently, best in O(n) time.

testCases.description: Basic - simple string with distinct chars

hints: Use a set to collect unique characters.
Remember to handle strings as-is (case-sensitive).

solution.javascript: function solve(param) { const s = (typeof param === 'string') ? param : String(param); return new Set(s).size; }

complexity.explanation: Each character is visited once and stored in a set to count unique characters.

title: Silent Word Counter

hints: Count word boundaries by scanning characters and detecting transitions from whitespace to non-whitespace.
Ignore multiple consecutive whitespace characters.

solution.javascript: function solve(param) { return 0; }
solution.python: def solve(param): return 0

complexity.explanation: Single pass scanning to count transitions from whitespace to non-whitespace.

title: Glyph Switchback
description: Given a lowercase string s, return the maximum length of a substring where vowels and consonants strictly alternate. Vowels are a, e, i, o, u. Strings contain only lowercase English letters. Time complexity should be linear in the string length; use O(1) extra space.
hints: Classify each character as vowel or consonant; look for longest run where types alternate.
Edge cases include strings with all vowels/consonants and single-character strings.

complexity.explanation: Single left-to-right pass classifying vowels/consonants; track the current alternating run length and the maximum.

title: Subarray Length Keeper
description: Find the maximum length of a contiguous subarray with sum not exceeding maxSum. All numbers are non-negative. Use a two-pointer/sliding-window approach. Implement solve(param) where param is [array, maxSum].
hints: Think in terms of a sliding window with a running sum.
Whenever the window sum exceeds maxSum, move the left pointer to shrink the window.

solution.javascript: function solve(param) { const arr = param[0]; const maxSum = param[1]; if (!Array.isArray(arr) || arr.length === 0) return 0; let left = 0, sum = 0, best = 0; for (let right = 0; right < arr.length; right++) { sum += arr[right]; while (sum > maxSum && left <= right) { sum -= arr[left++]; } best = Math.max(best, right - left + 1); } return best; }
solution.python:
def solve(param):
  arr = param[0]
  maxSum = param[1]
  if not isinstance(arr, list) or len(arr) == 0:
    return 0
  left = 0
  total = 0
  best = 0
  for right in range(len(arr)):
    total += arr[right]
    while total > maxSum and left <= right:
      total -= arr[left]
      left += 1
    best = max(best, right - left + 1)
  return best

complexity.explanation: Single pass with sliding window; left and right pointers only move forward.
